title: Retaking the hegemony of overseas national athletes has faced another crisis. Amid the strong perfor.. - 매일경제
content:
Retaking the hegemony of overseas national athletes has faced another crisis. Amid the strong performance of Korean players at the 45th GS Caltex Maekyung Open, Ian Snyman (South Africa), the only player from overseas to rank in the top 10, has built up his pride. Snyman cut four shots in the third round of the tournament held at Nam Seoul CC (par 71) in Seongnam, Gyeonggi Province on the 2nd, tied for seventh with a 5-under 208 overall. He is five strokes behind co-leaders Song Min-hyuk and Cho Min-gyu (hereinafter, 10-under 203). Of the 52 foreign nationalities from 18 countries who participated in the tournament, Snyman was the only one in the top 10 and created an opportunity to seek a come-from-behind victory in the final four rounds. Snyman, who started at the 10th hole on the day, made an eagle putt about 12 meters away from the 14th hole (par 5), and was able to reach the top 10 with a birdie putt that was difficult to climb down on the 9th hole (par 5). After the game, Snyman said, "I tried to play more conservatively than I did the day before. The strategy rather led to a better score. The biggest goal was to keep the ball under the hole all the time, most of which did that well. He said that there were about two mistakes, which led to a view of all of them. Snyman, who turned professional in 2018 and has been consistently active in the Asian Tour since 2020, has been an emerging dark horse, finishing second in the New Zealand Open last year. But he has never won a championship yet. The GS Caltex Maekyung Open also failed to make the cut in both 2024 and last year. Still, the GS Caltex Maekyung Open is meaningful to Snyman. Snyman's husband is Korean. "My sister came to Korea 15 years ago to teach English, and she met her husband here, got married, and now has two children," Snyman said. Snyman, who met her sister every time she participated in a competition in Korea, held a "surprise event" for her this year. Snyman said, "I brought my brother to caddie. As we don't see each other often in a year, I suggested to my brother, "Do you want to go to Korea together while caddying me?" and gladly accepted. The idea was to surprise my family so much that my parents in South Africa didn't even know it, but as soon as my sister saw my brother, I cried. I almost cried too," he said, revealing the behind-the-scenes story. This year's GS Caltex Maekyung Open, where three siblings are together in Korea, is a great help to Snyman. He said, "My sister has a good family here, but she misses her hometown. I was glad that my family could get together like this. I think it will be memorable for a long time to see my brother happy with each putt, he said. "That's how special this tournament is to me. Golf plays a role in connecting the family to the whole world," he said with a smile. Snyman, who is doing well in this competition with his family's support on his back, said, "I have no intention of changing the strategy I have used so far. The putting is really good. "I've been good throughout this tournament, so I hope you continue to make good putts on the last day," he said. ※ This article was translated using AI technology for reader convenience. title: Mars, Machavern Deliver Turner’s First Win in Nearly A Year - Sportscar365
content: Turner Motorsport claimed 50th GS win for BMW in Michelin Pilot Challenge at Laguna Seca… It took just three races for the new driver pairing of Dillon Machavern and Luca Mars, two past IMSA Michelin Pilot Challenge champions, to taste victory for Turner Motorsport and BMW as a collective unit. Machavern and Mars topped the GS class of Saturday’s WeatherTech Laguna Seca Raceway 120. They drove the No. 95 BMW M4 GT4 EVO to a 4.083-second triumph over the No. 2 CSM Porsche 718 GT4 RS Clubsport shared by Gordon Scully and Morgan Burkhard, with pole-sitter Nate Cicero and co-driver Robert Noaker taking third in the No. 13 McCumbee McAleer Racing Ford Mustang GT4. Machavern qualified eighth fastest but had moved up to third place behind the No. 13 Mustang and the No. 12 RAFA Racing Toyota GR Supra GT4 EVO2 when almost the entire GS field pitted under caution 56 minutes into the two-hour race. Mars exited the pits as the effective race leader, pursued by Noaker and Michael Cooper in the No. 44 Ibiza Farm Motorsport McLaren Artura GT4 that he took over from Moisey Uretsky. The three cars circulated in close proximity for most of the final hour as the race ran caution-free to the finish. In the closing stages, Mars was able to slightly progress away, and the pressure eased when Noaker eased his pace to save fuel and Cooper was forced to make a splash-and-go stop. Burkhard then passed a slowing Noaker for second place on the last lap. Mars was also conserving fuel, but it was not an issue in the end as he, Machavern, and Turner scored BMW’s 50th race win in GS class competition and 92nd overall in Pilot Challenge. The victory was the eighth in the series for Machavern and the fourth for Mars and the first for the team in series competition since Mid-Ohio last June. Mason Filippi and Bryson Morris co-drove the No. 33 Bryan Herta Autosport Hyundai Elantra N TCR to the TCR class win. It was a 1-2 finish for BHA, as Preston Brown and Denis Dupont rebounded from an early spin and a puncture to claim second place in the No. 76 Hyundai, a full lap behind their teammates. The Herta team now has five wins at three different tracks, the first team in history to do so. At Laguna Seca, the win joins its four previous triumphs in 2020, 2021, 2023 and 2025. The victory – which was the ten in Pilot Challenge competition for 2025 TCR champion Filippi and the second for Morris – was nowhere as easy as the result would indicate despite the fact that the No. 33 car basically led from the start to the checkered flag. Filippi took the pole and held the top spot through his first stint before handing off to Morris just prior to the halfway point of the 120-minute race. The biggest challenge to Morris came from the No. 99 Victor Gonzalez Racing CUPRA Leon VZ TCR, but Tyler Gonzalez, who shared the car with Franco Girolami, sustained a punctured left rear tire with 37 minutes remaining while running within a second of the lead and fell to seventh. Celso Neto and Rafael Reis then looked set to take second place in the No. 77 Stallion Motorsports w/GOU CUPRA, only for the car to stop on course with just two minutes to go. That left Dupont and Brown to collect the runner-up laurels, followed in third place by Karl Wittmer and LP Montour in the No. 93 MMG Honda Civic FL5 TCR. title: This Spider Launches at 130 G’s (Humans Black Out at 9) - AOL.com
content: Some predators chase. Others ambush. And then there are the ones that shoot. Across the animal kingdom, a surprising number of species have evolved ways to hit prey from a distance — firing water jets, venom sprays, sticky silk, or even microscopic harpoons. These aren’t gimmicks. They’re highly specialized hunting systems designed to solve one core problem: how to catch prey that is faster than the predator. Many rely on what scientists call latch-mediated spring actuation (LaMSA) — a way to store energy and release it instantly, like a biological trigger. The result is explosive speed, extreme precision, and in some cases, forces that rival human-made machines. From fish that snipe insects out of the air to snails that fire venom-loaded harpoons, these predators have weaponized the laws of physics. While many creatures, such as bombardier beetles and spitting cobras, “shoot” to defend themselves, these elite marksmen use their abilities offensively, actively hunting and capturing their next meal. If you thought fish only hunted underwater, the archerfish will quickly prove you wrong. This aquatic outlaw can knock insects off branches up to 5 feet above the water by firing a precise jet of water from its mouth. But it’s not just about force or power; it’s about physics and precision. Because light bends when it passes from air to water, the fish’s view of the insect is distorted. To hit its mark, the archerfish must account for this “refraction” before firing. This skill isn’t purely instinctual, either. Young archerfish practice to refine their aim, eventually achieving near-perfect accuracy, especially when they position themselves directly beneath their target. While many spiders wait for prey to get stuck in their webs, the spitting spider actively immobilizes its prey. In a fraction of a second, the spider sprays a specialized mixture of liquid silk and venom from its fangs while it rapidly vibrates its head, causing the silky streams to form a zigzagging net in the air. This sticky, toxic mesh pins insects to whatever surface they’re on. It’s fast, precise, and extremely effective. Unlike other eight-eyed spider species, spitting spiders only have six eyes. While most shrimp are scavengers, the pistol shrimp is a gunslinger of the sea. Unlike its relatives, this impressive hunter doesn’t kill by pinching; it uses physics to hunt. When the shrimp slams its oversized claw shut, it ejects a high-speed jet of water that creates a cavitation bubble. This bubble is a tiny pocket of low pressure that collapses almost instantly, triggering a massive release of energy. The pistol shrimp may only be 1.5 inches long, but it can move its claws at 60 miles per hour. The collapse of this bubble is so violent that it produces a shockwave louder than a gunshot, stunning or killing small prey from a distance. For a tiny fraction of a second, the temperature inside the collapsing bubble can reach nearly 8,700 degrees Fahrenheit (4,800 degrees Celsius). The energy release is so intense it actually produces a brief flash of light. The shrimp’s claw features a unique spiral, crack-resistant structure designed to withstand the repeated stress of these underwater “explosions.” Materials scientists are actively studying the pistol shrimp’s claw to develop stronger, more impact-resistant armor and helmets for humans. The velvet worm may appear soft, slow, and relatively harmless, but it is a surprisingly effective predator. Using specialized nozzles near its mouth, it sprays twin jets of slime at a speed reaching nearly 5 feet per second. The slime hardens in the air as the jets oscillate rapidly, and the movement ensures that the slimy webbing covers a large area to make it nearly impossible for an insect to escape. Once its prey is immobilized, the worm dissolves the victim’s insides and consumes the liquefied remains. Velvet worms typically hunt woodlice, crickets, and spiders. The worm also eats the dried strands of slime afterward, ensuring that no resources are wasted after the energy-intensive hunt. Unlike other spiders that build stationary webs and wait for prey to get caught, the ogre-faced spider actively casts its net like a tiny eight-legged fisherman. Hanging upside down from a single silk line, the spider holds a small, rectangular frame of highly elastic silk between its front legs. It uses its specialized slit sensilla or vibration sensors to detect the movements of insects walking or flying nearby. When an insect comes within range, the spider lunges, stretching the net to several times its original size and snapping it over the prey. Two of the ogre-faced spider’s eyes face forward and are much bigger than the rest, giving it excellent low-light night vision. The cone snail moves at a literal snail’s pace, yet its strike is one of the fastest and most lethal in nature. Instead of chasing its prey, this snail uses its hollow, venom-tipped tooth—essentially a built-in biological harpoon—to strike with incredible speed. It happens in less than one millisecond, even faster than the blink of a human eye. Once the harpoon hits, the snail injects a complex cocktail of neurotoxins that shuts down the prey’s nervous system instantly. Some cone snail species can launch their harpoon in less than 100 microseconds. Some species, like the geography cone (Conus geographus), are so toxic that a single sting can be fatal to humans. This species is sometimes called the “cigarette snail” due to a grim myth: if you are stung, you’ll only have enough time to smoke one last cigarette before you die. While this may be an exaggeration, the venom does act extremely quickly. Instead of spinning traditional webs, the bolas spider uses a specialized “fishing” technique to catch its prey. Instead of a net, it creates a single silk line tipped with a heavy and sticky glob called a bolas. The bolas’ incredibly sticky texture allows it to bypass a moth’s slippery scales and adhere directly to its body. The bolas spider creates silk with a sticky texture. ©Judy Gallagher / CC BY 2.0 / Wikimedia Commons – Original / License The spider releases a scent that mimics the pheromones of a female moth. When a male moth approaches in search of a mate, the spider swings the bolas in a circular motion to snag the moth out of the air. The chameleon is a long-range predator that relies on mechanical advantage rather than raw muscle power to catch its prey. Its long tongue doesn’t simply reach out to snatch up an insect; instead, the chameleon launches it from its mouth like a high-speed projectile. This mechanism relies on stored energy rather than rapid muscle movement, allowing it to function even in cooler temperatures. Chameleons have a unique physiology that allows them to “wind up” energy in their tongue’s tissue so they can snap it out at speeds of 60 miles per hour in a fraction of a second. The tip of the chameleon’s tongue has a suction cup-like pouch coated with saliva that is about 400 times thicker than human saliva. This special coating ensures that the bond is strong enough to pull the prey back, even if it is heavy or struggling. Rather than waiting for an insect to fly into its web, the slingshot spider transforms its entire web into a loaded spring. It constructs a conical web and pulls the center tight using a tension line attached to a nearby surface. By pulling back and holding the web under extreme tension, the spider stores potential energy, essentially turning itself into a living bow and arrow. When the spider senses prey, it releases the tension line, sending itself and its web catapulting forward at over 130 Gs (for perspective, humans typically black out at around 9 Gs). The sudden launch collapses the web over the prey, entangling it before it can react. Slingshot spiders shoot their own bodies at their prey. ©LOPEZ André / CC BY-SA 4.0 / Wikimedia Commons – Original / License The same silk structures that provide the tension also act as shock absorbers, bringing the spider to a safe stop at the end of its flight. The post This Spider Launches at 130 G’s (Humans Black Out at 9) appeared first on A-Z Animals.

title: Song Min-hyuk Jumps to Co-Lead With Cho Min-kyu at GS Caltex Maekyung Open - Seoul Economic Daily
content:
Six Birdies Propel Song to Top in Round 3
Cho Holds Lead Bogey-Free, Eyes First Title
Lee, Moon, Kim Aim for Final-Round Comeback Song Min-hyuk climbed into a share of the lead after the third round of the 45th GS Caltex Maekyung Open (total prize money 1.3 billion won), moving one step closer to his first victory on the Korea Professional Golfers' Association (KPGA) Tour. Song carded six birdies against a single bogey for a 5-under 66 in Friday's third round at Namseoul Country Club (par 71) in Seongnam, Gyeonggi Province. His 54-hole total of 10-under 203 lifted him from a tie for fifth after the second round to a share of the lead. A former member of Korea's amateur national team, Song made his KPGA Tour debut in 2024 but has yet to win. Having faltered in contention on several occasions, he has high hopes for this tournament. "I had many chances to win in the first half of last year, but I stumbled often while in contention," Song said. "I'm fine with falling 50 times. I think I can win if I get back up." He added, "If I play my own game and hold steady when I need to, good results will come." Cho Min-kyu, who shares the lead with Song, is a formidable opponent. After being in a tie for the lead a day earlier, Cho held his ground with four birdies and no bogeys on Friday. Cho, a two-time winner on the Japan Tour, has yet to capture a KPGA Tour title. His determination to win this event is particularly strong, as he has finished runner-up at the Maekyung Open three times — in 2011, 2020 and 2022. "A new opportunity has come. If I win, all the pieces will fall into place," Cho said. "My son was born on Monday, and for his sake, I want to step up as a father." The chasing group is also strong. Lee Tae-hee, who won this event in 2019 and 2020, is in third place at 7-under 206, three shots off the lead. Chasing a record third victory at the tournament, Lee will try to mount a comeback in the final round. Defending champion Moon Do-yeop and LIV Golf player Kim Min-kyu are tied for fourth with Kim Min-jun at 6-under 207. Kim Seong-hyun is tied for seventh at 5-under 208, while Wang Jeung-hun and Heo In-hoi share 11th at 4-under 209. Choi Chan, who claimed his first career win at last week's Woori Financial Championship, is tied for 17th at 3-under 210. Jang Yu-bin, who shared the lead a day earlier, dropped seven strokes on Friday to tumble into a tie for 45th at 1-over 214. AI-translated from Korean. title: BMW F 450 GS features image gallery - Autocar India
content: The F 450 GS replaces the discontinued G 310 GS as the brand’s entry point into the revered GS line-up. The quad-LED daytime running lamps are inspired by the flagship R 1300 GS. In terms of features, the F 450 GS has a 6.5-inch TFT display with connectivity, heated grips and adjustable levers. The 420cc parallel-twin liquid-cooled mill produces 48hp at 8,750rpm and 43Nm at 6,750rpm, paired with a 6-speed gearbox. The suspension offers 180mm of travel at both ends. The BMW F 450 GS is powered by a 420cc parallel-twin liquid-cooled engine producing 48hp at 8,750rpm and 43Nm at 6,750rpm, paired with a 6-speed gearbox. The motorcycle features a 6.5-inch TFT display with connectivity, all-LED lighting, heated grips, adjustable brake and clutch levers, and a height-adjustable rear brake lever. In terms of more safety-oriented electronics, it includes ABS Pro, engine drag torque control, traction control, Dynamic Brake Control and Dynamic Brake Light. The F 450 GS weighs 178kg and gets quad-LED daytime running lamps inspired by the flagship R 1300 GS. In India, the F 450 GS is available in three variants – Base, Exclusive, and GS Trophy. In addition to what's mentioned above, the Exclusive variant adds off-road foot pegs, hand guards, engine guard, Riding Modes Pro and BMW's Shift Assistant Pro for clutchless upshifts and downshifts. The GS Trophy variant features sport suspension, the Easy Ride Clutch system, aluminium engine guard, tinted Rallye windshield and the classic BMW tricolour paint scheme.

title: Song Min-hyuk and Cho Min-gyu occupied the top of the leader board of the 45th GS Caltex Maekyung Op.. - 매일경제
content:
Song Min-hyuk and Cho Min-gyu occupied the top of the leader board of the 45th GS Caltex Maekyung Open. Song Min-hyuk shot a 5-under 66 in the third round of the tournament held at Nam Seoul CC in Seongnam, Gyeonggi Province on the 2nd. Song Min-hyuk, who had an interim total of 10-under 203, tied for the lead with Cho Min-gyu. Lee Tae-hee, the only third-place player in the team, beat Lee by three shots. Song Min-hyuk, who started the game tied for fifth place, reduced five strokes by combining six birdies and one bogey. Song Min-hyuk, who tied for the lead, had a golden opportunity to win his first professional title at the GS Caltex Maekyung Open. "Overall, it was a good day," Song Min-hyuk said. "I'm happy to start the final day game tied for the lead." Song Min-hyuk, who won the tournament's best amateur in 2022 and 2023, when he played for the national team, is showing a strong performance at the South Seoul CC, where the GS Caltex Maekyung Open is held. The best record ever was the runner-up, and this year he is aiming for the championship. Song Min-hyuk said, "The competition I wanted to win the most since I was young is the GS Caltex Maekyung Open," adding, "I don't want to miss it as it's a difficult opportunity to catch." I will focus on it until the end so that I can continue the good atmosphere," he stressed. Cho Min-gyu, the runner-up of the tournament in 2011, 2020, and 2022, also did not hide his desire to win. Cho Min-gyu said, "It's not a victory that I can say I want to do, but I want to finish well," adding, "You shouldn't be in a hurry in South Seoul CC. I will calm down and do a good job on the final day," he said. Moon Do-yeop, the winner of last year's event, also inflated his hopes of winning for the second consecutive year. Moon Do-yeop, who posted a 6-under 207, tied for fourth. Kim Min-kyu, who plays at the LIV Golf Korean Golf Club, also did well. Kim Min-kyu, who is tied for fourth place with Moon Do-yeop, will try to win the come-from-behind victory on the final day. Kim Sung-hyun and Choi Jin-ho wrote a 5-under 208 to finish the third round tied for seventh. ※ This article was translated using AI technology for reader convenience. title: The GS Caltex Maekyung Open, "Korea's Masters," is held at Seongnam Nam Seoul CC on the first week o.. - 매일경제
content:
The GS Caltex Maekyung Open, "Korea's Masters," is held at Seongnam Nam Seoul CC on the first week of May every year. Naturally, many families visit the site ahead of Children's Day, enjoying a shot feast of top golfers at home and abroad, and eating delicious charcoal bulgogi lunch boxes to make a happy day. Indeed, it is a 'golf theme park'. In the third round of the 45th GS Caltex Maekyung Open held at Nam Seoul CC in Seongnam on the 2nd, families accompanied by young children stood out among the cloud galleries without fail. Children who are still unable to walk in strollers enjoyed various flowers and trees in the South Seoul CC while children who were interested in golf were also seen clapping vigorously looking at the players' shots. This year, to mark the 60th anniversary of Maeil Business Newspaper, the Kids Zone has been upgraded so that family galleries can watch and relax more comfortably. At the Kids Zone on the left side of the clubhouse, you can take commemorative photos with balloons emblazoned with the GS Caltex Maekyung Open logo and character balloons of various shapes, and it is gaining popularity by distributing sweet cotton candy. At the table with the parasol spread out, the whole family can eat snacks comfortably while avoiding the stinging sunlight. Children can also receive special gifts from golfers. It's an instant autograph ball event. It is an opportunity for children's fans to leave special memories at the golf course. After the players finish the game, they submit a score card, sign the golf ball immediately, and present it as a gift to children. The signed golf ball presentation event is receiving great response not only from children's fans but also from participating players. Players gave signed golf balls when children's fans were seen, and some players were willing to sign hats and clothes brought by children's fans in addition to the balls they prepared, and actively provided fan services while taking pictures and presented happy memories. Food, the specialty of the GS Caltex Maekyung Open, is also indispensable. Every time the GS Caltex Maekyung Open was held since 1986, charcoal bulgogi lunch boxes, which have been popular as a specialty of the South Seoul CC, were prepared in the third and fourth rounds this year. The taste and aroma of bulgogi cooked over charcoal spread around the course, leading the gallery's steps. ※ This article was translated using AI technology for reader convenience. content:

Draft picks in June: No. 39 (via MEM), No. 53 After losing in seven games to Golden State in last year's first round, Houston made a series of moves that were supposed to expedite its rebuild that started in 2020. Out went former lottery pick Jalen Green, veteran Dillon Brooks and draft capital. In came former MVP Kevin Durant and veterans Dorian Finney-Smith, Okogie and Clint Capela. The Rockets also extended Steven Adams and re-signed VanVleet, Holiday, Green and Tate. "I just think we're not a developmental team anymore," Rockets general manager Rafael Stone told the Houston Chronicle last summer. "Our goal is definitely to win a championship." But VanVleet tore an ACL before the season. Adams had season-ending ankle surgery in January. Finney-Smith and Capela were not part of the rotation. Meanwhile, Durant missed only four games during the regular season but played one game in the first round. (With Durant out, Houston in the series started the second-youngest lineup in NBA history: Reed Sheppard, Amen Thompson, Eason, Jabari Smith Jr. and Alperen Sengun.) Now, the Rockets enter the offseason with more roster questions. Houston doesn't have a 2026 first-round pick but has 10 players under contract for next season, including the starting five who finished the season. The team should also get a boost with the return of VanVleet, who has a $25 million player option for 2026-27. (He has until June 29 to opt in to his contract.) There are also decisions on former first-round picks Eason and Thompson. Eason is a restricted free agent and Thompson is rookie-extension eligible. And finally, what did the Rockets learn about their roster in the postseason games won without Durant? Before game six, the lineup of Sheppard, Thompson, Eason, Smith and Sengun was plus-18.6 points per 100 possessions per Cleaning the Glass. Durant is entering year one of a two-year $90 million extension that he signed last offseason. Extensions for Durant and Smith will leave Houston with $185 million in salary on the roster next season. The Rockets are projected to be $15 million below the tax and $24 million below the first apron. The availability of their $15 million non-tax midlevel exception is dependent on Eason's restricted free agency. It starts with evaluating whether Ime Udoka is the right coach for this roster. Houston won 52 games for a second straight campaign but went 22-23 in clutch games this season and was ousted by an undermanned Lakers team. The argument for Udoka is how Houston dealt with adversity after blowing a late lead in Game 3 and putting themselves in a 3-0 hole. Without Durant, Houston won two games with the same young lineup that started in Game 3. "It's just showing growth in certain moments and progress in certain moments," Udoka said after Game 5. "And I think we did that tonight." Next is to evaluate whether a healthy VanVleet can fix an offense that ranked 20th during the regular season and fourth worst in the playoffs. With VanVleet in the lineup last season, Houston ranked 12th in offense and went 26-18 in clutch games with VanVleet running the show. And, finally, what is the price point on Eason's next contract? He should get tendered a one-year, $8 million qualifying offer by June 29, which will then allow Houston to match any offer sheet this summer. Through January, Eason was averaging 12.1 points per game on 48% shooting and 48% on 3-pointers. But in 34 games since Feb. 1, Eason saw his averages drop to 9.2 points per game, 38% shooting and 26% on 3s. Thompson, the only Rocket eligible this offseason, can sign a five-year deal up until the day before the regular season. Thompson averaged career highs in minutes, points, assists and rebounds, and he trailed only the 76ers' Tyrese Maxey in minutes played this season. In Thompson's final 22 games of the regular season, he averaged 20.8 points, 8.2 rebounds and 5.5 assists per game. (For the season, 70% of his points were scored in the paint.) Defensively, opponents shot 42.8% when Thompson was the closest defender, per GeniusIQ. That ranked 15th among 188 players who defended at least 500 attempts in the regular season. A healthy VanVleet is a starting point, but other needs include shooting development from Sheppard, Thompson and Sengun. The Rockets could also use a 3-and-D wing -- unless Finney-Smith can fill that role -- and a reserve point guard who can facilitate and play off the ball. Houston has five open roster spots. The Rockets have their own first-round pick in the next seven years (starting in 2027) and can trade up to four. They have six second-rounders available. 2029: two most favorable between their own, Dallas and Phoenix 2025-26 record: 54-28

The logic was sound: improve the bench without going over the first apron or hampering their financial flexibility. Denver's offseason also featured trading for Jonas Valanciunas, signing veterans Bruce Brown and Tim Hardaway Jr. and extending starter Christian Braun's contract. The Nuggets lost every road game to the Minnesota Timberwolves while averaging less than 97 points. (Denver is 2-8 in its past 10 road playoff games.) And the Nuggets' offseason additions had little impact. In the first four games, Valanciunas was a DNP, and Johnson (22.2%) and Hardaway (33.3%) struggled from 3-point range. Denver enters the offseason with more roster decisions. Though Nikola Jokic, Jamal Murray and the other starters are under contract, Denver is pressed financially to retain Watson, Hardaway and Brown in free agency. All eyes are on the Nuggets' finances, as the franchise enters the offseason $18 million over the luxury tax and over both aprons. Denver has some financial flexibility because of Valanciunas' partially guaranteed contract. If the veteran center is waived by July 8, Denver is responsible for $2 million of his $10 million salary. "From what I understand, it was just a financial business decision [for the Nuggets]," Watson told the Denver Post in October. Because Watson is a restricted free agent, the Nuggets can match any offer sheet. The futures of reserves Hardaway, Brown and Jones are uncertain. Denver cannot sign any of the three players for more than 120% of the veteran minimum. Jones, who failed to reach a long-term contract during the season, started a career-high 37 games. Jones will become a restricted free agent if Denver tenders the forward a one-year, $2.7 million qualifying offer. Because he has early Bird rights, Denver can sign Jones to a contract up to $15 million but for a minimum of two seasons (not including an option). Jokic took a business-like approach on tabling extension talks last offseason. "I mean, I don't think about it," Jokic said at Nuggets media day in September. Because Jokic had two years remaining on his contract heading into 2025-26, he was eligible to sign a three-year, $201 million extension. But by waiting until this summer, Jokic can tack on another season worth up to $76.9 million. A new contract would put the three-time MVP under contract through 2030-31. "I want to put a team out there on the court that everyone is proud of," owner Mat Ishbia said last April. "It has to have an identity -- an identity similar to Phoenix. Some grit, some determination, some work ethic, some grind, some joy. We just haven't had that." To accomplish that, Ishbia veered from his game plan when he first took over. He promoted vice president of player programming Brian Gregory to GM. Gregory had one year of NBA executive experience and had been a college coach (Dayton, Georgia Tech, South Florida) from 2003 to 2023. Instead of targeting a veteran coach, as he did with Vogel and Budenholzer, Jordan Ott was hired. The 41-year-old had been an assistant since 2016 and coached for the Nets, Lakers and Cavaliers. As for the roster, there were no flashy moves similar to the Beal and Durant trades in 2023. Instead, Durant was traded to Houston for Jalen Green, Dillon Brooks and rookie center Khaman Maluach. Beal was bought out of his contract and waived. The unassuming offseason also included trading for center Mark Williams, signing two-way player Collin Gillespie and claiming Jordan Goodwin off waivers. The moves resulted in the surprise Suns winning 45 games and building a new defensive identity. But though the Suns improved, they struggled in the last six weeks of the season in clutch games. Odds at the No. 1 pick: 2% State of the roster: There is no team in the league that faces as many questions and decisions as the Warriors do this offseason. For the second time in three seasons, Golden State finds itself in the lottery. The Warriors have been a play-in team three out of four seasons since their 2022 championship and finished no higher than seventh in the Western Conference standings in three straight seasons.. While missing the playoffs can be attributed mostly to Jimmy Butler's season-ending injury and Stephen Curry playing his fewest games since 2019-20, the window to compete for a championship is closing if this roster is not healthy. Butler will be out for an extended period of time to start next season, recovering from a torn right ACL. He underwent surgery on Feb. 19. Before returning for the final three games of the regular season, Curry had been out since Jan. 24 with a right knee injury. Then there is the uncertain future of coach Steve Kerr. Kerr has led Golden State to four NBA championships since taking over in 2014-15, and his contract expires at the end of this season. Despite all of the uncertainty, expect GM Mike Dunleavy Jr. to explore every option on how to improve the roster. The Warriors traded for Butler last February when they were 25-25. "We're willing to do whatever it takes to improve this team, whether it's young players, first-round picks," Dunleavy said after the trade deadline. "We always have been, we always will be, as long as we're in this win-now window." The Warriors have the 11th pick in this draft and can trade three additional first-round picks; 2028, 2030 (if 1-20) and 2032. They are allowed to swap firsts in all seven years, as well. What could present a challenge in the Warriors' roster reconstruction is their inability to cobble together enough salary for a major acquisition, should a player like Giannis Antetokounmpo become available, and still have flexibility adding to the roster. Because of the lack of sizable contracts on the roster outside of Curry's and Butler's, Green's $27.7 million salary would be required if Antetokounmpo or another All-Star became available. Curry, Butler and Green constitute 79% of the Warriors' payroll next season. That leaves them with six players (Moses Moody, Brandin Podziemski, Gui Santos, Horford, Will Richard and Melton) each earning less than $13 million. Green, Horford and Melton have a player option and cannot be traded, unless they opt in to their contract. For the first time since the 2019-20 season, Golden State is projected to be under the luxury tax. Including the player options of Green, Horford, Melton and their 2026 first-round pick, the Warriors are $14.5 million under the tax, $22 million below the first apron and $35 million under the second apron. The three with player options have until June 29 to opt in. Because of the first apron hard cap, it is unlikely Golden State can use the $15 million non-tax midlevel exception and still have flexibility to remain under once the roster is filled out. Building a roster that can compete for a championship around a 38-year old franchise player in Curry. But can Golden State take a win-now approach and still build the roster for when Curry, Butler and Green are no longer around? The delicate balancing act starts with Kerr. Kerr has proved that he can win with a veteran-laden roster. But is he willing to take on the arduous challenge of coaching a roster when Curry is no longer on it? Before the season, Kerr told the media that he was comfortable playing out this season and dealing with his future in the offseason. "I'm very comfortable just going into this season with a year left [on the deal]," Kerr said. "I'm so aligned with [GM] Mike [Dunleavy] and [owner] Joe [Lacob]. There's no reason for discussion [or] concern. This is kind of a point in our relationship where it's like, let's just see how it is at the end of the year." Curry is eligible, starting on Aug. 29, to sign a two-year $136.7 million extension. The new contract would pay him the highest per-year salary in his career ($65.7 million and $71 million) for his age-39 and age-40 seasons. Other than breaking his left hand in the 2019-20 season and missing 58 games, Curry hasn't suffered a significant injury that cost him more than 12 games until the right knee injury this year. "You'd start running and doing your normal [rehab] workout," Curry told ESPN's Anthony Slater, describing the problem. "[Then] toward the end of however long the session was, you'd start to feel the pain creep back in and the next day it'd be awful. Played that song and dance so many times over the last two months." Curry ranks eighth among all active players in career minutes played. The 36-year-old Green, for his part, has been the heart and soul of the Warriors' past championships, and he too is extension-eligible. "Maybe you opt out and do a longer deal. Maybe you opt in and extend," Green told ESPN. "I earned the right to be in this position. I won't misuse it. I won't abuse it. I never have. You don't get 14 years out of a place if you were abusing it. All of that s--- matters." As for the team's post-Curry future, former lottery picks James Wiseman, Jonathan Kuminga and Moody were supposed to form a new core. Wes Edens, the team's controlling owner until April 2028, spoke matter-of-factly when he talked to ESPN's Ramona Shelburne about the future of Milwaukee's franchise player. "Giannis is going into the last year [of his contract]," Edens said. "So one of two things will happen: Either he will be extended or he'll be traded." Starting Oct. 1, Antetokounmpo is eligible to sign a four-year, $275 million contract. (If he exercises his $62.8 million option for 2026-27, it would become a three-year, $213.6 million deal.) But to sign a fourth extension with Milwaukee, Antetokounmpo has to be sold on the franchise's plans to bolster a roster that will miss its first playoffs in a decade. The search process for the next head coach could look different than when Milwaukee targeted veteran coach Doc Rivers to take over a roster that included Antetokounmpo and Damian Lillard. Due to the uncertainty of Antetokounmpo's future, GM Jon Horst will need to cast a wide net of coaching candidates. The Bucks will likely add a top-10 pick in this year's draft -- due to the Jrue Holiday and Damian Lillard trades, the Bucks do not control their own first-round pick until 2031 -- and have 12 players from the current roster under contract for 2026-27. Five of those players, including starting guard Kevin Porter Jr., have a player option. The Bucks enter free agency $15 million below the luxury tax and $20 million under the first apron, including their first-round pick. Milwaukee could gain additional relief if Porter, Trent, Harris, Prince and Sims decline their player options and sign elsewhere. The five players have $21 million in combined salary for next season, and the deadline to opt into their contracts for next season is June 29. The Bucks also have until June 29 to exercise Nance and Jackson's team options. If last summer and this regular season taught Milwaukee anything, it is ownership and the front office should have a strategic three-year blueprint on what the roster would be like without Antetokounmpo. As ESPN's Shams Charania reported, Antetokounmpo expressed serious doubts and concerns about the roster before the season. Those concerns should only accelerate with the state of the Bucks roster heading into the offseason after how the season ended. Antetokounmpo's last game was March 15 due to a hyperextended left knee. Despite the injury, Antetokounmpo told Milwaukee he was healthy enough to play, but the Bucks did not medically clear him, causing another rift. "I'm available to play, but I'm not in the game. I'm available to play today. Right now. I'm available," Antetokounmpo told reporters. For the betterment of the organization long term, Milwaukee should not be in a holding pattern on Antetokounmpo, but rather aggressively exploring trades leading up to the draft. An Antetokounmpo trade would require interested teams to weigh multiple factors before creating a package to land the two-time MVP: How much leverage does Antetokounmpo have in choosing his next team? Would teams trade a hefty collection of assets -- draft picks, young players on controllable contracts and sizable short-term deals -- with no guarantee on an extension? Antetokounmpo would be eligible for the same extension he could sign with the Bucks, albeit not until six months after a trade. Milwaukee has another decision to make. When the Bucks signed Ryan Rollins last offseason, they included a player option in his contract for 2027-28. Rollins had a breakout season and could become a free agent next summer; he is not allowed to sign an extension this season. As Bucks general manager, Jon Horst spent the past few offseasons retooling around Antetokounmpo to this point. Now as the head of basketball operations, he would be faced with building a roster without their franchise player for the first time. The Bucks have either traded previous first-round picks or prospects they drafted, so there are no players eligible for rookie extensions. The only players extension-eligible are Antetokounmpo, Jackson and Kyle Kuzma. Closure with the future of Antetkounmpo is a starting point, but Milwaukee needs a lead guard and an identity on defense. The Bucks went from 12th in defense last season to 27th this year. From the Holiday and Lillard trades, Milwaukee has no control of its first-round pick until 2031. It is allowed to trade up to two first-rounders (2031 and 2033) and can swap first-rounders in three years (2031, 2032, 2033). The Bucks have one second-round pick available. title: All-time championship competition is taking place at the 45th GS Caltex Maekyung Open. On the second.. - 매일경제
content:
All-time championship competition is taking place at the 45th GS Caltex Maekyung Open. On the second day, Lee Tae-hee, Jang Yoo-bin, Shin Sang-hoon, and Cho Min-gyu were tied for the lead, and 47 players were within a five-shot gap. The third round, in which the main character who will lift the trophy, will be held on the 2nd at Nam Seoul CC in Seongnam-si, Gyeonggi-do. Lee Tae-hee, Jang Yoo-bin, Shin Sang-hoon, and Cho Min-gyu showed the best performance on the first and second days. The four players, who recorded 6-under 136 for two days, dominated the top of the leaderboard. The most notable name is Lee Tae-hee, the two-time winner of the GS Caltex Maekyung Open. Lee Tae-hee, who is also the main character of the tournament's second consecutive victory for the first time in history, is trying to win three times. Lee Tae-hee, who hit 6 under par on the first day, wrote even par on the second day. Lee Tae-hee was satisfied even though she failed to reduce the number of pars. This is because he remained in the lead without shaking even in the face of strong winds. Lee Tae-hee said, "I had five bogeys, but I recorded even par with one eagle and three birdies," adding, "As the shot and putt are not bad, I am looking forward to the rest of the round. I will prepare well and finish the tournament well," he said. Jang Yoo-bin, who returned to Korea after playing in LIV Golf last year, showed his performance in 2024, when he won the Korea Professional Golf Association (KPGA) Tour Genesis. "The current shot is similar to 2024, when golf is the best," Jang Yoo-bin said. "If the putt goes well, I think we can perform better than tied for fourth in 2024 and tied for eighth in 2023." Shin Sang-hoon and Cho Min-gyu also did not hide their desire to become the champion of the GS Caltex Maekyung Open. The two players expressed their determination to engrave their names on the list of winners of this tournament. The two players vowed to "do their best to compete for the championship in front of the cloud crowd." Kim Min-kyu and Kim Sung-hyun, who are active in LIV Golf and the PGA Tour, also did well. The two players tied for fifth place with 5-under 137. Moon Do-yeop, the winner of last year's event, tied for 13th at 4-under 138. Moon Do-yeop, who lost one shot on the first day and was on the verge of missing the cut, hit 5 under par on the second day, setting the stage for his second consecutive victory. The result of deciding to pass the cut was decided to be one over 143. The gap with the co-leader group is seven shots. As we don't know what will happen in the difficult South Seoul CC, all the players who made the cut will try to raise the ranking on the third day. ※ This article was translated using AI technology for reader convenience. title: The "luxury shot competition" for the 45th GS Caltex Maekyung Open trophy will heat up over the week.. - 매일경제
content:
The "luxury shot competition" for the 45th GS Caltex Maekyung Open trophy will heat up over the weekend. During the competition, the venue's specialty "Cham charcoal Bulgogi Lunch Box" has also been preparing since Saturday morning to attract the gallery. On the terrace of the Nam Seoul CC Club House in Seongnam, Gyeonggi Province on the 2nd, charcoal-grilled meat covered the area around the golf course from early morning. Galleries, players, and officials who were passing by the nearby charcoal-grilled meat scent that stimulated the nose stopped walking once in a while. Charcoal bulgogi lunch boxes will be on sale from 10 a.m. on the 2nd and 3rd. Although you may think of it as "a charcoal rib at a golf course clubhouse," the South Seoul charcoal bulgogi lunch box is a specialty of the GS Caltex Maekyung Open, which boasts a long history. It was in 1986 that this lunch box made its debut. Later, when the GS Caltex Maekyung Open was held at Nam Seoul CC, charcoal bulgogi lured galleries without exception. It reminds me of the Pimento Cheese Sandwich, a specialty of Augusta National GC in Georgia, the United States, where the Masters Tournament is held. Lunch boxes are only presented on weekends when the 3rd and 4th rounds are held. Charcoal bulgogi lunch boxes have become very popular every year among golf fans. There were times when up to 500 were sold a day, and at lunchtime, there is a rare scene of waiting in line to buy lunch boxes. If you want to eat delicacies at the golf course with your family and enjoy the "two birds with one stone" effect of watching the fierce shot competition of top professional golfers at home and abroad, the South Seoul CC is perfect this weekend. ※ This article was translated using AI technology for reader convenience. title: BMW F 450 GS review: GS WHAT! - BusinessLine
content:
BMW F 450 GS
																															| Photo Credit:
											Kaizad Adil Darukhanawala BMW Motorrad’s GS lineup is easily its most prolific one, popular in India as well as the rest of the world; the G series had a single-cylinder engine, the F series carries parallel-twins, while the R series features BMW’s famous boxer-twins. It’s a combination of history, versatility and desirability that enables BMW to sell this family of do-it-all motorcycles by and to the droves. And now, we have the new F 450 GS, a motorcycle that replaces the G 310 GS while also superseding it in almost every aspect. Designed by BMW but made by TVS Motor in India, the F 450 GS is a premium Motorrad machine in terms of build quality and equipment. It gets a 6.5-inch TFT screen connected to a rotary-scroll dial on the left side of the handlebar, a typical BMW touch, and the terminologies used in it are the same as the bigger GSes, too. There’s an X-shaped headlight with all-LED elements, a detail that again connects it directly to its bigger siblings. The clutch and alternator covers are made of magnesium alloy which helps keep the motor’s and overall weight down. And there are three variants: Base ( ₹4.7 lakh), Exclusive ( ₹4.9 lakh) and GS Trophy ( ₹5.3 lakh). I rode the last one, which comes with all the bells and whistles, including an easy-ride clutch (ERC). A lot more on that later. Headline numbers are as follows: a brand-new 420cc parallel-twin with a 135-degree crankpin offset makes 48 bhp at 8750 rpm and 4.42 kg-m at 6750 rpm, both sent to the road via a 6-speed gearbox. The motorcycle weighs 178 kg with a 14-litre fuel tank and a seat height of 845 mm. It comes with 19-inch front and 17-inch rear wheels with 100/90- and 130/80-section tyres, standard sizes for all-purpose motorcycles. Suspension travel stands at 180 mm at both ends, with a ground clearance of 220 mm. And BMW claims a top speed of 165 kph for it, which is more than is necessary in the real world. Among electronic rider assists are a ride-by-wire throttle, a quickshifter, four ride modes (Rain, Road, Enduro and Enduro Pro), traction control, dynamic-brake control, engine drag-torque control and lean-sensitive ABS. Most of these features are standard across the variants, though some additions are available only on the Exclusive, while the GS Trophy gets all of them and then some. The F 450 GS also comes with heated grips as standard across all variants which, I think, is a bit too much in a mostly superheated country like ours. And now for the most fun part. Out on the road, far away from glorious spec-sheet claims and sky-high expectations, the F 450 GS left me nonplussed. Yes, it felt like a full-size motorcycle, roomy and comfortable yet manageable. But, for me, the ERC took away a significant part of the involvement of riding a motorcycle. Was it for a beginner, an expert or someone in between? It’s a centrifugal clutch that engages at 2700 rpm; until then, at a standstill, you can row up and down the gearbox without touching the clutch lever, and the motorcycle won’t budge. Indeed, in the spirit of journalistic inquiry, I even made a standing start in sixth gear and the F 450 GS simply rolled away, whining like a big scooter. This is probably why the clutch-lever action is on the harder side; the internals necessarily have to be stouter to deal with less-than-considerate riding. And after the screen is done flashing a message to shift to a lower gear, it’ll tell you that the clutch is overheating, too. I didn’t see the latter, though. After putting the ERC at the back of my mind, the F 450 GS seemed more like a normal motorcycle. Most apparent was how it felt so light and nimble, turning and tightening its lines at will. The brakes were strong and inspired a lot of confidence; the front brake had a very sporty and powerful feel to it, not something usually seen on a dual-purpose machine. However, what came as a surprise was that the F 450 was not exactly GS-plush; even the previous G 310 GS was cosseting in its ride quality. Sure, the F 450 GS’s suspension was strong enough to bash through everything in its path, but it transferred quite a lot of residual impacts to my body. That’s not very GS-like, I must repeat, though it was always super-stable at all speeds. Even with the GS Trophy’s adjustable suspension, which I didn’t fiddle with, I don’t see it becoming a two-wheeled cross-country sofa. I suppose it’s just a matter of getting used to it if you buy it. As for the engine, I was again left with mixed feelings. I left it in Enduro mode for the duration of my ride, with the second-last level of safety assists, and it came across as quite a lively motor. It had a bit of a high-rpm-screamer quality when I really twisted the throttle, though the overall sound was nothing exceptional, even if it was a properly quick and fast motorcycle. Nonetheless, it was fairly smooth for the most part, though vibrations did surface at various points, especially after 6500-7000 rpm. At a steady 100 kph, though, it sat at a fuss-free 5500 rpm which bodes well for long-distance riding. And it remained smooth and manageable at low speeds in traffic as well, the only niggle continuing to be the CVT-like rubber-band effect of the ERC. Overall, the F 450 GS proved to be a motorcycle that’s extremely easy to ride across a range of conditions. I only rode it off-road briefly, but I gathered that it has the hardware and electronics to acquit itself respectably in moderate off-road conditions, too. The gearshift quality, even with the quickshifter, was on the notchier side; I expect it to improve as it gets bedded in. Better tyres would be at the top of my to-do list if I ever bought the bike. Also, the quality of stickers on the bike wasn’t what I was expecting on a bike in this segment, let alone a BMW. And the lack of colour options is a letdown, too; the Base and Exclusive only come in black, while the GS Trophy is the brighter one you see here. However, with that 14-litre tank, range should easily be upwards of 300 km, which is decent enough for long hauls. But basic competence is not the point of motorcycles like these in 2026, is it? In the end, despite possessing all the right details, the F 450 GS lacked the sense of occasion that should come as standard equipment on such a motorcycle. I never once felt that I wanted this motorcycle right away. Its demeanour felt a bit out of character for a GS, honestly, and I expected it to be more than it is right now, especially given what it asks for. Does it change the mid-size game for BMW? Oh, yes. But for the segment? That’s a bit more complicated, since it doesn’t set any outright standards on its own. Also, the prices I mentioned earlier are introductory, so they will be revised upwards at some point. And BMW service costs do have a reputation of leaving wallets so empty, they echo. I’m not so sure a motorcycle made in India should be replicating what imported machines find unavoidable. But of course, there’s no denying that the F 450 is a GS — after all, it comes with those BMW roundels that are impossible to brag or argue against. If you’ve been waiting for a relatively affordable Motorrad, this is the only option you have. Not much of a choice, if you ask me. (221220-K) SEOUL: South Korean rising star Yubin Jang moved a step closer to a breakthrough victory at the GS Caltex Maekyung Open after carding a two-under-par 69 on Friday to claim a share of the halfway lead at Namseoul Country Club. The 23-year-old, regarded as one of Korean golf’s brightest talents, sits at six-under-par alongside compatriots Mingyu Cho, Sanghun Shin and two-time champion Taehee Lee in a tightly packed leaderboard, with only eight shots separating the leaders from the cut line. Cho and Shin both returned rounds of 70, while Lee signed for a 71 as the quartet headed into the weekend tied at the top. A group of eight players are just one shot behind on five-under, including New Zealand’s Nick Voke, who fired a superb 66, as well as Korean duo Minkyu Kim, a two-time Korea Open champion, and PGA Tour player Seonghyeon Kim. Jang, who competed for Iron Heads GC in the LIV Golf League last season, said the experience gained on the global circuit has helped elevate his game. “Having played on LIV Golf across a variety of courses and conditions, I found it relatively easy to adapt to the windy conditions today,” said Jang. “That experience has been a great help. Even in situations where I might normally take a more aggressive club, I’m now trying to play more steadily. I do feel that I’ve grown as a player.” Jang enjoyed a stellar 2024 season, topping the Korean Tour Order of Merit and earning Player of the Year honours, achievements that helped secure his LIV Golf opportunity. He was also part of South Korea’s gold medal-winning team at the Asian Games in 2023. “Since my days on the national team, the tournaments I’ve most wanted to win are the GS Caltex Maekyung Open and the Kolon Korea Open,” he said. “I was in contention in both 2023 and 2024, but it was disappointing not to win. Those missed opportunities have stayed with me, so I’ll do my best to play without any regrets.” Meanwhile, Voke surged into contention after making six birdies in a bogey-light round of 66. “We were feeding off each other,” said Voke after playing alongside Cho and Frenchman Julien Sale, who also carded a 66. “Very pleased with the round. Challenging golf course, but challenging conditions as well. This wind is tricky, it’s strong, it’s coming from all sorts of directions.” Defending champion Doyeob Mun also made a strong move with a 66, bouncing back from an opening 72 to sit at four-under-par heading into the weekend. If this kind of company isn't your style, you like companies that generate revenue, and even earn profits, then you may well be interested in Goldman Sachs Group (NYSE:GS). title: GS Architecture retrofits Hampstead Passive House with "fabric-first approach" - Dezeen
content:
Local studio GS Architecture has completed its renovation and extension of a family home in Hampstead Heath, London, updating it to meet low-energy Passivhaus standards. Named Hampstead Passive House, the semi-detached Victorian home was overhauled to create a more comfortable, light-filled dwelling with significantly reduced energy consumption. GS Architecture set out to achieve the rigorous Passivhaus EnerPHit standard, which certifies low-energy buildings that have been refurbished with high levels of insulation and airtightness. It upgraded the house with improved insulation and triple-glazed windows, while introducing solar panels, low-energy lighting and a mechanical ventilation and heat recovery system. "Sustainability was central to the brief – the clients were committed to creating a home that performed as efficiently as possible, and Passivhaus EnerPHit offered a clear and ambitious benchmark for the project," GS Architecture partner Guy Stansfeld told Dezeen. "A fabric-first approach underpinned the design, with careful attention given to insulation, airtightness and breathability," added Stansfeld. "The use of wood fibre insulation and traditional lime plaster was key, allowing the existing building shell to be made airtight while still breathable." As part of the project, GS Architecture replaced the home's existing conservatory and extensions with a new timber-framed addition. Topped with a wildflower green roof, this rear extension houses an open-plan kitchen and dining space, with timber doors opening out into the garden. Inside, the space combines a variety of timber elements, including the kitchen island and cupboard fittings, the dining table and a built-in bookcase. Timber finishes appear alongside reclaimed materials such as old snooker-table slate used for the kitchen worktops, complete with original brass studs and pocket cutouts. Other reclaimed and repurposed elements include salvaged floorboards used to line the extension's ceiling and for internal door facings, while cupboard doors are made from former cheese-manufacturing boards. According to the studio, new materials and finishes were carefully selected to avoid petrochemical-based products. Clay from the rear garden was mixed into the lime-based paint for the walls, contributing to its natural look. A wide opening connects the kitchen-dining room to a sitting room beyond, complete with the same pale timber flooring, natural palette and matching bookshelves. Working alongside GS Architecture, designer Boris Vervoordt introduced a palette based on the Japanese design philosophy of Wabi-sabi, which celebrates imperfection. Existing features were preserved to retain the home's character, including the original curving timber staircase. Upstairs are three bedrooms, complete with the same pared-back palette, with pale timber cupboards and floorboards, and a neutral colour scheme. "Texture plays a leading role in shaping the atmosphere of the home," said Stansfeld. "Lime plaster walls create a subtle sense of movement and warmth," he described. "Natural materials such as timber, slate and limestone add depth and variation, while the colour palette remains grounded and earthy." In the bathrooms, dark tiles are paired with dark Tadelakt walls and ceilings, a traditional Moroccan waterproof plaster, chosen by the studio for its natural breathability. Other homes designed to Passivehaus standards recently featured on Dezeen include a house in Chile featuring multiple courtyards and "the largest Passive House building in Canada". title: The Fastest Gravel Tire in the World Just Got Bigger (and Probably Faster) - Outside Magazine
content: French tire manufacturer Hutchinson has added new gravel tires to its collection with the Touareg Race, Touareg Race GS, and Caracal Race GS. Additionally, there will be a new size for the Caracal Race, as the full line will extend up to 50 mm. The new tire models might grab the headlines, but the 50mm Caracal Race is the real story. Across multiple independent tests, the existing 40mm and 45mm versions have already proven to be some of the fastest gravel tires on the market. In drum testing by Bicycle Rolling Resistance, the Caracal is the second-fastest 40mm+ option available, trailing the Rene Herse Snoqualmie Pass Extralight by just 0.1 watts. Field testing by John Karrasch using the Chung Method backs this up, crowning the 45mm version the outright fastest tire across Category 1 and 2 gravel. Those figures suggest the new 50mm option will capitalize on that same speed, while using the extra volume to improve puncture protection and rolling efficiency over rougher terrain. Compared to the Caracal Race, the Touareg has more treaded tire that provides consistent traction throughout the entirety of the tire instead of just along the side knobs. This will reduce the overall speed of the tire slightly, but it should allow for a more predictable ride and increased handling compared to the fairly slick Caracal Race. While the Touareg is not a new tread for Hutchinson, the compound has been completely overhauled with the Mach Tread 3.0 compound, delivering 25% greater rebound and 10% higher tear resistance to the tire. That change to the compound will allow the tire to be used at lower pressure while still retaining the same responsiveness and confidence over uneven terrain. Add in the SwiftEasy casing to reduce weight and improve the flexibility of the tire, and the Touareg carries over most of the same elements of speed found in the Caracal Race in a lightweight 480g tire. “Gravel racing has evolved into a discipline defined by pure performance, data, and marginal gains,” Martin Chuchu, Hutchinson Product Manager, said of the development. “At Hutchinson, we recognized that no single tyre could meet the demands of every modern gravel race. With the Caracal Race established as our fastest gravel tyre, we developed the Touareg Race to complete the system.” The Touareg and Caracal tires are also billed as not necessarily an either/or proposition. Hutchinson argues that the best tire system for gravel racing with the new and improved line-up is the Touareg up front and the Caracal in the rear to balance speed and traction demands neatly. Both of these gravel tires also offer additional options that utilize Hutchinson’s GridSkin construction. GridSkin is a knitted mesh reinforcement used to provide extra sidewall protection while still offering the flexibility needed to handle the imperfections of off-road riding. These new gravel tires are the first in Hutchinson’s line to use the technology, which has been a staple of the brand’s road line. Currently, the Hutchinson lineup is available in Europe, but expect to see it in North America soon. You can check out more information on the full range at the Hutchinson website.

title: Lunchbreak: 5 Vikings Draft Choices Spotlighted in ESPN’s Top 100 ‘Picks, Steals, Fits’ - Minnesota Vikings
content:
Two-hundred-and-fifty-seven college football standouts realized life-long dreams last weekend. In the days since the 2026 NFL Draft concluded, we've watched hundreds more join teams as undrafted free agents, including 19 on the Vikings. We've also observed initial report cards from external draftniks and noted brief assessments earlier this week of Minnesota investing twice in run-game reinforcements. Another post-draft recap worth highlighting came out Wednesday: ESPN draft analyst Matt Miller's 100 best picks, fits and steals of the event — and to spoil the surprise, Minnesota was a recurring awardee. The Vikings are one of five teams on Miller's sheet with five "favorite" selections last weekend, along with the Chargers, Colts, Jets — whose three first-round picks were ranked 5, 6 and 22 — and Ravens. That's the majority of Minnesota's nine-player draft class, in case you hate math. The team's fourth pick and first offensive player chosen this year, former Northwestern tackle Caleb Tiernan, appeared first for the Vikings at 34 in Miller's ranking. Tiernan was Miller's 61st prospect available and was drafted 97th overall, confirming Minnesota obtained great value in the third round. Miller touted Tiernan as the potential "long-term plan at right tackle" considering starter Brian O'Neill will turn 31 years old in September; granted, O'Neill still is an exceptional player. Although Tiernan primarily played on the left side in college, Miller shared that scouts think he's equipped to man the right side or even switch to guard. At 43, Miller applauded the Vikings top choice of former Florida defensive lineman Caleb Banks. Miller wrote the following of the club's highest-drafted defensive lineman since Erasmus James in 2005: The biggest surprise of Round 1 — more than [quarterback Ty] Simpson (drafted 13th overall by the Rams) or [receiver Carnell] Tate (drafted 4th by the Titans) — was Banks finding his way into the top 20. Foot injuries sidelined Banks throughout last season and in the pre-draft process, but he should be seen as an immediate starter when healthy with Jonathan Allen and Javon Hargrave being released this offseason. The middle of the Vikings line was low on talent, and Banks is easily the most traits-rich player on the roster. Based on value, this pick is a big reach, but it fills a massive need. And the high-end potential from Banks makes it understandable in a draft class that was low on starting-level defensive tackles. Minnesota's other big addition to the defensive front, former Iowa State defensive tackle Domonique Orange, made the cut, too, popping up at 68 on Miller's list. Ironically, Orange was Miller's 68th prospect. Miller opined the Vikings were smart to circle back to their defensive line and take "Big Citrus," reasoning, "The return on investment will be huge if they can get two starting-level defensive tackles on rookie contracts." Like Banks, Miller added, Orange should have every opportunity to start in Week 1. At 83 and 85, Miller spotlighted Minnesota's new backfield tandem, award-winning former Michigan fullback Max Bredeson (Miller's 126th prospect, picked 159th) and speed-possessed former Wake Forest running back Demond Claiborne (Miller's 160th prospect, picked 198th). These choices are widely liked! Miller explained Bredeson was "invaluable to the Michigan offense" because of his ability to align in different spots. Miller elaborated with an obvious excitement: "He had no peers in the class as a blocker. If there's going to be a power run game in Minnesota, Bredeson is the fullback you want kicking it off." As for Claiborne, Miller sees him as "the lightning to the thunder of veteran Aaron Jones, Sr., and Bredeson," and he projects that Claiborne's pass-catching value on third down will be apparent quickly. Of course, Minnesota also has Jordan Mason, who played 16 games last season and had 159 carries for 758 rushing yards and six touchdowns. Miami is the only club with greater representation on Miller's list, garnering his stamp of approval six times: LB Jacob Rodriguez (Round 2-Pick 43 overall) at 14, WR Chris Bell (3-94) at 31, T Kaydyn Proctor (1-12) at 35, CB Chris Johnson (1-27) at 38, LB Kyle Louis (4-138) at 40 and edge rusher Max Llewellyn (7-237) at 93. Click here to read Miller's entire article and decide for yourself which franchises perhaps aced the draft. The Athletic's Alec Lewis supplied perspective Thursday about each of Minnesota's draft picks. With insights from college coaches and staffers, Lewis guided us from Banks, who former Florida head coach Billy Napier described as "a generational height, length, twitch and intelligence type of guy who also has a competitive streak that makes him different," to Day 3 center Gavin Gerhardt out of Cincinnati. You can read Lewis' full story here, and we will share some of the most interesting intel below. Starting in reverse order, Cincinnati's former director of player personnel Jack Griffith told Lewis that Gerhardt is "Probably the best servant leader I've ever been around," and added, "He could've left a few different times, but he was the linchpin who brought the cultures together between (Bearcats former head coach) Luke Fickell and (Head Coach) Scott Satterfield. He's the guy you trust to make all the calls." Maybe the best quote in Lewis' piece is attributed to Bill O'Boyle, who was Tiernan's offensive line coach in 2024 at Northwestern. Boyle mentioned Tiernan overcomes shorter arm length with hip mobility, and he listened closely to him teaching the Wildcats linemen how to use independent hands in pass blocking. Lewis stated that O'Boyle believes Tiernan's ceiling depends on opportunity. "We could put in a screen game where we could get him on the perimeter in a way you couldn't with other tackles," O'Boyle relayed to Lewis. "Because he's that fast. He's got that sneaky, fat-man speed." One other player worth mentioning is the fourth defender Minnesota picked in the Top 100 this year: safety Jakobe Thomas, who played under former Gophers defensive coordinator Corey Hetherman in the same role last season at Miami. Hetherman has studied Brian Flores' wizardry and identified overlap between what Thomas did for the Hurricanes and what he could potentially do for the Vikings. "His style of play, and their style of loading the box and doing different things with the safeties and being multiple, is ideal," Hetherman told Lewis. "He was also a guy who didn't come in until August (after playing at Middle Tennessee State), and he picked it up quickly. So, you'd think he'd do the same there." Canes safeties coach Will Harris, who helped coach Byron Murphy, Jr., at Washington, raved about Thomas' play style: "We always talked about enforcing your will on your opponent, and he did that." content:

The 2026 GS Caltex Maekyung Open Golf Championship purse is set for ₩1,300,000,000 ($880,409), with the winner's share coming in at ₩234,000,000 ($158,473) -- the standard 18 percent payout according to the Asian Tour's prize money distribution chart. The GS Caltex Maekyung Open Golf Championship field is headed by Jeunghun Wang, Jbe Kruger, Chase Koepka and more of the Asian Tour's best players. The 144-player field is the sixth event on the Asian Tour schedule and is the latest event of the 2026 season. A cut is made after 36 holes to the top 65 players and ties for the final two rounds of the tournament. All players who finish four rounds of the tournament will earn money. The event is played this year at Namseoul Country Club in South Korea. Beyond money, there are important points, perks and benefits on the line for the field -- in particular, the tournament winner. The winner of this event will get add to their Order of Merit tally, which is crucial for access to tournaments and bonuses. The winner gets 8.9 Official World Golf Ranking points, with the points available based on field strength. Winning comes with other big benefits, including a two-plus season exemption on the Asian Tour. Ryan Ballengee is founder and editor of Golf News Net. title: Logitech G’s Racing Wheel for PlayStation and Xbox Gets Multiple Price Cuts on Amazon, Perfect Driving Game Set-Up for Less - Kotaku
content:
This article is part of Kotaku Deals, produced separately from the editorial team.
      We may earn a commission when you buy through links on the site. If you’re a huge racing game fan and you’ve been looking for a way to upgrade your gaming set-up, but haven’t found the right way to do it, then we’ve got the perfect deal for you. After all, what could be better than investing in an amazing racing wheel and then really locking-in whether you’re trying to take pole position, come out on top in Forza, or you’re just really into kart racers. While there are a lot of good wheels out there, the Logitech G RS50 Racing Wheel is one of the best, which is why you’d normally have to pay $700 for it. That’s a lot of money, but today on Amazon it’s on sale with a 25% discount, which brings the price down to $525 instead. That’s still a lot, but this thing is top-of-the-line for a reason. Both the PlayStation and Xbox variants are at the same price today, although if you’re looking for one of these for your gaming PC, then you can actually grab either one with no issues. This thing boasts some seriously impressive specs, and delivers Direct Drive force feedback thanks to TrueForce, which means that you’ll feel the wheel fighting you as it would in an actual race, and it has a max torque of up to 8 Nm. Not only that, but with a 2,700-degree max rotation, you can really go for it on those corners for a level of realism and immersion that most other wheels just can’t compare too. The wheel itself is 11-inches, and has 13 buttons along with adjustable paddle shifters, once again helping you to feel like you’re really in the car. All you need to do is attach it to a surface and then sit back and get driving, and you’ll never look back. You can even customize it further if you want to spend a bit more money, with a few of the parts being easy to swap out for other Logitch Racing pieces, meaning that this is something that can be what you want, when you want, and it’ll grow with you as get better at using it. This is an incredible deal, but it’s also one that’s not likely to last for very long. If you can afford it while it’s down to $525, then this is the perfect chance to buy it, because there’s no way of knowing when it’ll be on sale again, and missing out this time could sting. title: BMW R1300 GS Adventure Dark Edition revealed - Visordown
content: BMW Motorrad has revealed a new R1300 GS Adventure Dark Edition, but sadly, it’s only available for French customers. BMW Motorrad France has done it again, this time unveiling a new R1300 GS Adventure Dark Edition to go along with the recent special edition F900 R and F900 XR models. The F900 R and F900 XR special edition models received Frozen Grey (F900 R), and Aurelius Green (F900 XR) liveries, with both motorcycles using the German company's car division for inspiration. Like those two models, the new R1300 GS Adventure Dark Edition is for the French market only, which was also the case regarding the special edition F900 GS and R1300 GS models that were revealed last year. The R1300 GS Adventure Dark Edition will be sold in France for €22,900, which at current exchange rates converts to around £20,000. That also makes it €1,700 (around £1,470) more than the standard R1300 GS Adventure model in France. The new special edition variant will be available to buy until 30 June. It includes a Triple Black style with black spoked wheels, heated rider and passenger seats, a high tinted windshield, tinted deflectors, black-painted scoops on the tank, and an alarm. Just like the standard model, the R1300 GS Adventure Dark Edition gets Dynamic Cruise Control as standard, as well as other features such as Dynamic Traction Control, ABS, four riding modes (Eco, Rain, Road, Enduro), heated grips, and additional LED spotlights. Alongside the R1300 GS, the standard Adventure model is currently one of BMW’s two most popular motorcycles. In terms of total sales in 2025, the R1300 GS Adventure was the top-selling model with 33,570 units sold, compared to the R1300 GS’ 32,555. Both models were also very popular in the UK throughout 2025, although it was the GSA that spent most months at the top of the adventure sales charts. The GSA was the UK’s best-seller in seven out of 12 months last year. title: Gen.G's 'Strong Upper Half' Leads to 2-1 Victory Over Resilient Kiwoom DRX - Inven Global
content: In the 2026 League of Legends Champions Korea (LCK) Spring Split match held at LoL Park in Jonggak on the 30th, Gen.G Esports defeated Kiwoom DRX 2-1. While Kiwoom DRX put up a valiant fight, the series ultimately showcased the superior individual skill and 'weight class' of Gen.G's top side. Gen.G dominated from the first set. Their mid and bot lanes pulled ahead during the laning phase, and the bot lane's balance completely collapsed following intervention from 'Canyon' Kim Geon-bu's Jarvan IV. Leveraging their lead, Gen.G monopolized objectives and snowballed the game. 'Chovy' Jeong Ji-hoon's Annie quickly racked up six kills, and Gen.G took the first set with a 10,000-gold lead. Kiwoom DRX started the second set on a high note, with 'Willer' Kim Jeong-hyeon's Wukong securing consecutive kills in the mid and bot lanes. The Wukong grew to carry-level strength, and despite a brief response from Gen.G's bot duo, the Wukong remained a constant threat. Turning their attention to the top lane, Kiwoom DRX repeatedly shut down 'Kiin' Kim Gi-in's Jayce and picked up additional kills in skirmishes around the top side. Gen.G, however, was not a team to fold easily. Trailing by over 4,000 gold, they executed a brilliant team fight to secure a massive win and take Baron. Although Kiwoom DRX maintained the lead, they were pushed to the brink when 'Chovy's' Akali picked off their bot duo. Yet, around the 29-minute mark, a team fight broke out before Akali could join; Kiwoom DRX successfully focused down 'Ruler' Park Jae-hyuk's Jhin, secured a decisive victory, and took the second set. In the deciding third set, 'Kiin's' K'Sante dominated 'Rich's' Shen in the laning phase, effectively neutralizing Kiwoom DRX's strategy centered around the Shen. Without even securing a kill, the gold gap between the top laners widened to nearly 1,400. To Kiwoom DRX, the fed K'Sante was an immovable mountain. Even when they managed to catch out an opponent to initiate a fight, they lost consecutive skirmishes due to the sheer presence of the K'Sante, who refused to go down. Around the 25-minute mark, Kiwoom DRX finally took down the troublesome K'Sante. They claimed the Dragon as a reward and finished off 'Ruler's' Jhin, who was caught in an overextended position. However, Gen.G responded by picking off 'Andil' Moon Gwan-bin's Nautilus and securing Baron, further widening the gap. Kiwoom DRX tried to hold on, banking on the late-game carry potential of 'Ucal' Son Woo-hyeon's Viktor, but it was an uphill battle with both nexus turrets already exposed. Ultimately, Gen.G secured the third set victory through a backdoor play by 'Chovy'.
