According to a release, the title launched globally on April 29th and has surpass DXRacer has been named the Official Gaming Chair Partner of MSL Thailand, the professional Mobile Legends: Bang Bang (MLBB) league in Thailand. According to a release, the partnership will see DXRacer Riot Games announced that the 2026 VCT Pacific Stage 1 would enter its second week from April 10th-12th at SOOP Sangam Colosseum in Mapo-gu, Seoul, with playoff positioning and Championship Points alr Riot Games has unveiled a new structure for the VALORANT Champions Tour (VCT) from 2027, replacing its current league system with a tournament-driven model featuring open qualification to Masters and Riot Games has added Akali to 2XKO, introducing the League of Legends champion alongside a new Local Duos feature and continued support for community-run competition. title: Titan at Caltech, forgotten in India: The science genius who made architecture cool - India Today
content: In the grand story of modern engineering, some names echo loudly while others quietly shape the world from behind the scenes. Guruvayur Subramanian Ramaswamy belongs firmly to the latter. A pioneer who fused physics with architecture, Ramaswamy redefined how large structures are built, yet remains largely unknown outside academic circles in India. Born in 1923, Ramaswamy approached buildings not as stacks of bricks or steel, but as systems governed by invisible forces. His intellectual journey took him to California Institute of Technology (Caltech) during its golden era, where he trained under some of the world’s leading minds in structural and fluid mechanics. It was here that he began to see design through the lens of physics, especially the power of curvature. Inspired by something as simple as an eggshell, Ramaswamy asked a radical question: why do we rely on heavy materials when nature achieves strength through shape? His answer transformed engineering. He pioneered the concept of funicular shell structures, using principles like the catenary curve, the natural shape formed by a hanging chain, to design roofs that were astonishingly thin yet incredibly strong. A funicular shell structure is a thin, curved roof or surface designed so that forces flow naturally through it, mainly as compression. Inspired by shapes like a hanging chain turned upside down, it uses minimal material yet remains very strong because the shape carries the load efficiently rather than relying on thickness. Instead of fighting gravity with bulk, Ramaswamy’s designs worked with it. By aligning structures along the natural flow of forces, he proved that strength could come from geometry rather than sheer mass. The result: vast industrial roofs, domes, and hangars that used far less material without compromising stability. Back in India, he became the founding director of the Structural Engineering Research Centre, helping establish the country as a global leader in shell structures decades before such ideas became mainstream in the West. His work enabled cost-effective construction, a critical need for developing nations. Yet, Ramaswamy occupied an unusual intellectual space. To engineers, his work seemed too theoretical; to physicists, too applied. This made him a ghost figure, influential, but not widely celebrated. His seminal book, Design and Construction of Concrete Shell Roofs, became a global reference text, shaping projects from the United States to Russia. He was awarded the Shanti Swarup Bhatnagar Prize, one of India’s highest scientific honours, and served as a consultant to the United Nations, advising countries on low-cost, high-efficiency construction. Yet his recognition largely remained confined to professional circles. Next in who after the Bibha Series? He used the math of an eggshell to build the roofs of the future. Dr. Guruvayur Subramanian Ramaswamy (1923-2002) was the Ghost who decoded the physics of curvature. While the world was using heavy steel & thick concrete, he was using Pure pic.twitter.com/EeYR7CgFVI— Parimal (@Fintech03) April 30, 2026 To his family, he was a disciplined academic. To the world, he was a quiet revolutionary. Today, the sweeping roofs of factories, warehouses, and public spaces, structures that seem to float effortlessly, owe much to his ideas. And yet, there are no statues, no public memory, no widespread recognition of his name in India. Ramaswamy’s legacy is not carved in stone, but embedded in it. Every thin shell that stands strong against gravity is a testament to his belief: that the smartest structures are not the heaviest, but the most intelligent.

title: The adventure bike that refuses to age! - Motorcycle News
content:
By Adam Child and Adam Oldfield
Updated: 30 April 2026 If you decide you fancy a 2017-2018 BMW R1200GS adventure bike, you’d better be prepared to do some serious research first. There are versions upon versions to choose from. In the road-biased side, you can opt for the standard model, a TE or a TE Exclusive. If you fancy tackling the dirty stuff, there’s the Rallye and the Rallye TE. Then of course there’s the BMW R1200GS Adventure, which comes in a bewildering choice of five different spec levels of its own. The standard BMW R1200GS came with handguards, pannier fastenings and switchable riding modes. If you stepped up to the TE you got electronic suspension (ESA), cruise control, ABS, dynamic traction control (DTC), a better onboard computer and a whole host of other goodies. The TE Exclusive has the same specs as the TE, but came in “Iced Chocolate” paint and gold calipers (a staggering £305 addition at the time). Like this story? You can get more MCN in your life by clicking here and asking Google to make us a preferred source. The Rallye is the off-road-biased version of the R1200GS and came with sporty upgrades to the pegs, seat, screen and wheels. You also get automatic stability control (ASC), radiator and frame guards and knobbly tyres. The Rallye TE got you all of that, plus DTC, LED lights… still reading? There’s a complete list of features in the ‘Equipment’ section of the review below. This generation's ESA and DTC increased both comfort and safety, while the automatic pre-load adjuster made a huge difference if you are one for always adding luggage or a pillion. At a time when you thought the GS couldn’t get any more advanced, it did. BMW improved on perfection and made their flagship GS smoother and more refined than ever. The Rallye version takes the fear out of off-roading and bridges the gap between the standard GS and the big Adventure. It goes as well as it looks, is superbly purposeful and was a popular variant with adventure riders, but in full spec it was and still is an expensive bike to risk riding over rocks. The 2017 update brought Euro4 compliance without sacrificing performance (125bhp and 92.2ft-lb), plus drivetrain revisions including a damper and gearbox refinements for smoother shifts. Most importantly, BMW introduced its next‑generation self‑levelling Dynamic ESA, lean‑sensitive traction control and cornering ABS. At its launch, MCN praised the refinement improvements and the evolution of the electronics package. In group tests against the Ducati Multistrada 1200 and KTM 1290 Super Adventure, the GS wasn’t the most powerful machine, but it was repeatedly highlighted for comfort, throttle connection and real-world pace. In MCN’s 600-mile Wales test, it was described as “the nicest place to be” at anything up to 90% of performance. Owner feedback over time only praises and reinforces that reputation. High-mileage examples are very common, and long-term testers have covered thousands of miles in mixed use without major mechanical failures. The GS’s reputation has evolved from class pioneer to benchmark all-rounder, and even at nearly a decade old, it still defines what many riders expect from a big adventure bike. Remarkably modern, in fact. The self‑levelling Dynamic ESA still feels sophisticated, automatically adjusting preload according to load and maintaining geometry under braking and acceleration. Compared to the previous model’s system, it delivers noticeably better ride quality over uneven roads and more composure mid-corner. The boxer engine’s broad spread of torque makes it effortless on A‑roads and motorways alike. It lacks the explosive top-end of its KTM and Ducati rivals, but that deficit actually makes it easier to use consistently. The throttle connection remains one of the most satisfying, allowing an earlier, smoother drive out of corners. Ergonomically, it still works superbly. The spacious riding position, wide bars and plush seat make it the most comfortable bike in its class during long-distance tests. The only dated element today is the analogue dash, which lacks the visual drama of modern TFT systems, but it remains clear and functional. Overall the 2017 GS feels evolved rather than outdated; it still delivers an outstanding ride and can compete with the latest but doesn’t feel like you are miles away from a brand-new machine. The R1200 boxer engine enjoys a strong durability reputation, with many examples exceeding 50,000–70,000 miles. Regular servicing and recall compliance are more important than low mileage. Servicing can be costly at a dealer, but many tackle the job themselves, at least for the minor service. Annual servicing costs are reported around £200–£280 depending on service type. Owner reports show generally high satisfaction with occasional corrosion complaints on exposed metal parts. Engine cases and throttle bodies have been replaced under warranty in isolated cases. A standard service takes place every 6000 miles or once a year depending on use. It should include a new engine oil filter and oil change, a diagnostic check and a brake inspection. Major services, every 12,000-18,000, include all standard service items mentioned before, plus a valve clearance check and adjustment, replacing spark plugs, replacing the air filter, and checking and/or replacing the final drive oil. Current used price range is £7600–£10,000 as of May 2026, with variation between the specs and trim packages. Check out MCN’s Bikes for Sale for the latest R1200GS deals. Overall, the TE and Rallye variants command stronger prices, but you can expect to find many examples fully loaded with factory-fitted kit. High-mileage models can be found at a lower price; if there is a regular documented service history to back up that it has been cared for, it should easily deliver many more miles. Compared to it's class rivals, the BMW R1200GS resale value remains strong. Further proving the claim that the GS model range is the sought-after adventure bike. In short, yes! It remains one of the most complete all-round motorcycles ever built. While newer machines offer more power and flashier technology, few deliver the GS’s combination of comfort, composure and real-world usability. If you want drama and explosive acceleration, look elsewhere. If you want a dependable, mile-devouring machine capable of tackling everything from commuting to the odd gravel track, the 2017–2018 R1200GS is still an outstanding buy. For 2017 BMW added clever self-levelling next-generation ESA and improved the DTC. Previously you could change the damping on the move to soft, medium and hard, but on the 2017 GS the damping is adjusted automatically, adapting to your riding by reading numerous sensors such as lean angle, brake pressure, speed, pitch and more. In basic terms the ESA is always re-adjusting automatically and keeping the bike level. Brake heavily into a turn, and it will increase damping at the front; accelerate hard, and it will increase damping on the rear. Over bumps and imperfections, the ride is truly impressive. You have two choices: the standard road version or the off-road version, which means opting for off-road-biased rubber. You can then add sports suspension, with increased spring rate, longer struts and extended travel that push the seat height to 900mm, 20mm higher than standard. The taller Rallye with sports suspension increases the ride height and improves the bike's off-road abilities. You have to make allowances for the off-road rubber and tall stance on the road, but it makes perfect sense off-road. The new self-levelling ESA suspension means all the damping is done automatically and pre-load is automatically adjusted (at a standstill). Off-road, the new GS Rallye complements the average rider, while more inexperienced riders can rely on the clever off-road-biased rider aids. However, if you ride hard enough, the ESA makes it hard to feel the tyres working in corners. But you soon learn to trust the cornering ABS or the excellent DTC, which is lean angle sensitive. The partially water-cooled boxer motor remains unchanged for 2017 despite now being compliant with Euro 4. This means peak power is still 125bhp@7700rpm with peak torque of 92.2 ftlb@6500rpm. With its excellent fuelling and drive, BMW made a big step with the boxer engine when they updated it in 2013. Resale values are high because BMW’s reliability and service are top level. This GS continues their high level of quality, feeling robust and secure. Our BMW R1200GS owners' reviews show some very fond owners, but also those who say the bike itself and looking after it are too expensive. In June 2017 BMW made a change to fix a fault with the GS 1200's fork stanchions. Bikes built before this should have been fixed under warranty when a recall was issued. At just over £15,500 the GS TE Exclusive came fully loaded, and the styling and colours gave an even greater impression of sophistication. The base Rallye started at £12,730, which was good value for money, but our test bike was priced at £18,318 at the launch event – even now that’s an expensive bike to bounce over rocks. First published on 6 September 2017 in MCN by Adam Child In 2017 we carried out an adventure bike mega test to crown our bike of the year in that category. It means pitting the BMW R1200GS against its F800GS stablemate, the Ducati Multistrada 950 and 1200 siblings and the KTM 1090 Adventure R. The sheer breadth and depth of its versatility and skills means that the KTM 1090 Adventure R is our adventure bike of the year. It dominated the off-road sections, and it didn’t let itself down on the tarmac, either. It’s light, fun and punchy, has excellent brakes and is reasonably comfortable. At just over £12,000 it’s not smashing the budget either. Second spot goes to the Ducati Multistrada 1200 Enduro. It has more gadgets, more power and more rider aids as standard but is also the most expensive on test. Close behind is the BMW R1200GS in third. It’s great for touring and easy to ride on and off-road. Then comes its smaller brother, the F800GS. – it’s punchy, roomy and easy to ride with real off-road potential. And that leaves the Multistrada 950. It may have won its class shootout but was outgunned in this test. At £15,564 the TE Exclusive on the test at the launch came fully loaded with a new style package, Dynamic Traction Control, Dynamic ESA, LED headlights, keyless ignition, daytime riding lights, hill start control, on-board computer, shift assist, Dynamic riding modes, GPS preparation, chrome exhaust, heated grips, tyre pressure control, cruise control, hand guards, LED indicators, ABS Pro and fairing fasteners. The standard Rallye comes with radiator and frame guards, wide enduro pegs, a single seat, a sports windscreen, cross-spoked wheels, riding modes, ASC, hand guards and fairing fasteners. The Sport comes with additional Dynamic traction control, LED headlights, daytime riding lights, hill start control, additional dynamic riding modes, chrome exhaust, heated grips, tyre pressure control, LED indicators and ABS Pro. Our Rallye test bike had also been ridden through the accessories catalogue and was equipped with the additional premium package (which cost £1645 in 2017), which included Dynamic ESA, keyless ignition, onboard computer Pro, GPS bracket and cruise control. Furthermore, the sports suspension added £290 and the lovely-sounding Akrapovic exhaust adds another £860. Then add the engine protection (£322), cylinder head covers (£188), headlight guard (£87), adjustable back brake lever (£111), and milled HP levers up front (£95 each), and the prices start to add up. The standard model started at a more palatable £12,730 but if you bought a bike based on the spec of the test bike we rode at the time, it totalled a whopping £18,318! in 2017, even now it's a fair wedge. I’m not a monogamous rider. It doesn’t matter how good a bike is, I really struggle with the concept of only having one. I currently own four (a Ducati 996, Suzuki GSX-R750 ‘Slabby’, Honda VFR800FiW and Honda CB750 SOHC), but if you backed me into a corner and threatened me with the concept of singl… 8 owners have reviewed their BMW R1200GS (2017 - 2018) and rated it in a number of areas. Read what they have to say and what they like and dislike about the bike below. Of all the five water cooled GS’s I’ve owned since the first one in 2013, this last incarnation is by far the best of the 1200 LC engines & the quickshifter has been improved over time from the first versions in 2014 The ESA suspension makes the bike. It’s smooth over bumps & gives great ride quality. Brembo brakes are strong & work well with aftermarket pads from Brembo which are cheaper than BMW original ones. I don’t ride with a pillion but the handling is not affected with the full vario luggage attached. I strip & clean the calipers twice a year to keep the brakes sharp The engine seems to get better with age & I ride in a group who have newer 1250s but I don’t get left behind. The shift cam engine has some power improvements but in real world riding it doesn’t make much difference in my view. Apart from servicing & consumables like tyres & brakes, nothing has failed or gone wrong in 71000 miles The engine cases & throttle bodies were replaced  under warranty due to corrosion but are corrosion free now some four years on I ride around 12000 miles annually so need two services a minor 6000 & a major 12000 one Continental TKC 70’s have been my tyre of choice from the start. Good grip in all weathers & the longest lasting of any tyres I have had before on my previous bikes. The additional lights from the adventure model are a real benefit to safety as they do give the bike more visible presence. Crash bars are  a good addition & protect the cam covers in the event of a low spied drop. A sidestand extension is good as it helps stop sinking on softer surfaces..I’ve fitted an aftermarket dual USB charge socket so I can keep my phone charged. Cruise control helps on motorway journeys to take strain off the right hand Buying experience: Bought it new from Allan Jefferies at Rotherham. This was the last 1200 model anywhere  in the UK and was the display model at BMW HQ. I owned the bike for just over three weeks, rode it 5 times and broke down twice. Kept going into limp mode and losing power. Both throttle bodies replaced but I then asked the dealer for my moneyback. Got my money and bought a brand new V-Strom. At least it won't break down Most of it you don't need in the real world. Quickshifter was fun Buying experience: Bought used from a BMW dealer. Very low miles on a 2017 plate and paid £12,750 which was over the odds Great bike , easy to ride fast or slow, very comfortable. Depends on which service, but after owning Ducati’s anything seems cheap Expensive to buy and maintain but worth that bit extra Buying experience: Better than expected and a cut above other dealers I sold an R1150RT to buy this bike, and I miss my old RT.  Having said that, this GS is a lot more comfortable on long rides but I do feel I'm sitting ON the bike rather that IN it like the RT.  The engine is light years ahead of the RT in terms of refinement, but it does rev higher than the RT- 3500rpm against 4000rpm at 70mph.  Handling on the GS is phenomenal, on my RT, I would always be aware of the sheer bulk when cornering 'low and slow' but not on the GS !  I now hunt down those same corners and roundabouts. Mine has the quick shifter but I never use it so can't comment. LED headlights are fantastic and the spot lights will burn holes through steel if held too close.  All in all, I've made a good choice and I love riding my GS Sensational braking, but a bit too sharp on initial bite. If your not too careful you'll find yourself engaging all the relevant safety systems.   The ride quality has to be experienced to be appreciated, it really is an amazing bike to ride. Smooth, comfortable and a feeling of complete  safety. Its a fantastic engine and the power curve is useful throughout the range. I'm not interested in revving the nuts of engines, otherwise Id have bought something else. One gripe I do have though, is my bike sounds very tappety. Not what I expected on this engine at 4000 miles. I shall have a poke under the covers in the spring. Small amount of corrosion on the main stand after just 4000 miles..not good for a bike costing this much. As another reviewer has said, the switchgear seems very cheap. On my old RT, it felt like it would last forever. On my GS it feels like its come from a Nintendo switch and I can actually see exposed wiring going from the loom to the left switch gear. I haven't had it serviced yet, but will be doing my own maintenance. I've bought a diagnostic / coding tool to clear any faults or servicing codes. Im not expecting anything major to go wrong. I bought my bike slightly used. It has every conceivable extra, but my favourite toy is the TFT screen and the BMW Nav 6.  I love being able to switch riding modes on the move as well. I got caught in a downpour last summer and switched to rain mode in 2 seconds at 65mph, then back again when the sun came out and the roads dried. Akrapovic exhaust makes a lovely burbling sound on the over run. The original BMW panniers are top quality, but hold a lot less than it looks like they should. Buying experience: Bought privately for 12k. Original owner paid almost 19k and lost 7k in two years. Comfortable and easy to ride but vibration through bars that should have been sorted years ago Corrosion shouldnt happen on a bike that costs this much Buying experience: Bought from dealer, sales guys spot on, service dept a joke I have been riding motorcycles 34 years now, this is simply the best motorcycle I have ever ridden, certainly the best I have ever owned. A GS is no speed machine but there is torque everywhere, I have no doubt it could tow a trailer,. It's main selling point for me however is comfort I'm 6' 2" and i'm probably on the short side for it, the riding position is just brilliant, leg room, wide bars and a mega comfy seat, it is an ergonomic masterpiece, a real mile muncher. There are only 3 things wrong with this bike; 1keyless ignition, I hate this with a passion, I wouldn't be so distrusting of it if it were at least possible to turn the key off, 2 switches are shockingly cheap for a bike of this price range, when I was given the demonstrator I went back in the shop and told them the switches were broken and I didn't want blaming for it, when he came out and checked he said " no, that's normal.", you really do need to experience for yourself how bad they are. Finally, no.3, they aren't very exciting, if you want the thrill of motorcycling, this isn't the machine for you. Cornering ABS, suspension adjusts automatically, even when a pillion gets on or off, no dive when braking. Ride quality and brakes are this bikes forte. A real touring bike. Also I know all bikes lose their weight as soon as they are rolling but for a bike weighing nearly a quarter of a ton it is really flickable, it is much more like a 600cc class bike in the handling department. Not fast by motorcycle standards but loads of grunt, also very smooth throttle/power delivery. Expensive bike, but you can easily see where the money went. Never had any trouble with it at all, just coming up to 2 years ownership. £100 run in service and £150 first year service, I thought servicing was very cheap considering it's BMW, plus you can book a loan bike to go home on free of charge. When I went to look at this bike, I fully intended to buy the base model and thought all the extras were just gimmicky toys. The demonstrators are obviously all fully specced, within the first ten minutes of riding I'd used about half of them and realised I wanted them all. Favourite things, hill hold, electronic suspension gives an unbelievably smooth ride and really exceptional TFT screen. The quick shifter can be a bit clunky on the up change, gets smoother with more throttle, down shifts are lovely with an auto blip on the throttle.
As an aside, I have a SENA intercom in my helmet, it will only connect to one none SENA device at a time so if you want instructions from the navigator 6 (sat nav), you cannot connect your helmet to your bike, which automatically connects to your phone, so no phone calls or music. Even though the sat nav is connected to the bike and operated by controls on the bike it is a separate bluetooth connection, it is possible to connect phone to sat nav but you have to go into menu's on the bike and sat nav separately which is a five minute faff about you could do without, first world problems. Buying experience: Bought brand new from a dealer, Alan Jefferies in Rotherham. BMW customer experience is second to none, salesman was honest about everything up front, I was trading in a bike less than a year old and we came to a favourable arrangement on both sides. Great bike - bought 18 months old with 10k. on clock from a Motorrad dealer in Tunbridge Wells - Cooper. for £12k   Did not come with luggage - bought full set for £1000 -well worth it .   Impressed with built in Navigation on TFT to be used with the BMW app and easy to pair .  Do not feel worth spending £600 on Garmin 6 sat nav though bike is prepared to take it - find use of phone based nav is fine .   Automatic tyre pressure adjustment great feature if pillion and variable luggage loads and all very intuitive .  Smooth ride (had come from a 30 year old R80 RT so it was transformational !  No regrets about spending this money - wish had done sooner.  Am nearly 66 and retired and bike is easy to handle - when its in movement and particularly in slow traffic it is very easy to handle.  TFT screen is great with all the information very visible - speed ; revs; gear engaged etc..   Absolutely no bad points   Screen protection is good, surprising, as came from a barn door screen on the R80. love the bike - worth getting a good padlock and chain and a cover when parking tremendous spec on the TE Exclusive   could not want for more ... title: 500 free gelato cups at Tofu G’s new outlet - Yahoo Lifestyle Singapore
content: Korean dessert brand Tofu G is expanding into Singapore’s CBD with a new outlet at UE Square, ready to delight with its signature tofu-based gelato. If you are in the area on 1 May 2026, there is a good reason to swing by early. To celebrate the grand opening, the brand is treating customers to 500 free cups of its newest creation, Pomegranate Tofu Gelato. It is a 1-day, first-come, first-served event, with each person limited to 1 cup. The new Pomegranate Tofu Gelato is the star of the launch. It has a fruit-forward profile, balancing sweetness with a slight tarty edge for a refreshing taste. It works well with the tofu base, cutting through the creaminess and leaving a clean finish. If you prefer something more familiar, the menu still includes staples like Vegan Tofu Gelato, Vegan Rich Chocolate Gelato, Vegan Black Sesame Gelato and Pistachio Gelato, all priced at S$8 per scoop. The concept draws on Korean tofu-making techniques, giving the brand a distinct identity in a dessert scene packed with options. It is entirely vegan, emphasising high-protein, low-fat ingredients. The gelato menu leans lighter on the palate, making it an easy pick for those who want dessert without the heaviness of traditional gelato. Everything is made in-store, giving each batch a fresher taste and a consistency that feels carefully crafted rather than mass-produced. If you miss the opening day giveaway, there is still a reason to return. From 2 to 8 May 2026, customers who purchase a Pomegranate Tofu Gelato can enjoy a free double scoop in another flavour. This simple deal makes it easier to sample more of the brand’s offerings in 1 visit. With its central location and a concept that feels different from usual dessert spots, Tofu G’s newest outlet is well-positioned for quick office breaks and after-dinner treats. If you are curious about tofu-based desserts, this might be the place to start. For a full list of locations, opening hours & their contact number, refer here. Newly-opened TOFU G at TRX brings Malaysia’s 1st tofu gelato from SG to KL The post 500 free gelato cups at Tofu G’s new outlet appeared first on SETHLUI.com. It was brother against brother at the end of the 2026 Kentucky Derby. Bettors on longshot winner Golden Tempo won $48.24 on a $2 bet. Antonelli won two straight races before F1's unexpected April hiatus. The horse, and its owner, are going home with a nice pile of money.
